title: Environment-Enhanced Single-Photon Absorption in a Nano-Ring of Dipole-Coupled Quantum Emitters
authors: Eric Sánchez-Llorente, Helmut Ritsch, Maria Moreno-Cardoner
abstract: Decoherence is mostly detrimental in quantum information and quantum optics applications. However, the interplay between environment-induced incoherent dynamics and unitary evolution can give rise to novel quantum many-body phenomena that can be harnessed as a useful resource. As is well known, in dense subwavelength atomic arrays only a single collective eigenmode in the single-excitation manifold couples strongly to free-space radiation, exhibiting superradiant spontaneous emission. Most of the remaining eigenstates form a manifold of weakly radiative modes, giving rise to long-lived subradiant excitations. Here we demonstrate that populating these subradiant modes via additional decoherence mechanisms, such as dephasing or coupling to phonons, can significantly enhance single-photon absorption in a nanoring of quantum emitters. Such nanoring geometry is particularly appealing due to its unique optical properties and its resemblance to natural light-harvesting complexes, which serve as efficient antennas in photosynthesis. Our findings may shed light on fundamental aspects of energy absorption in nature; despite the much greater complexity of biological systems, they may nonetheless operate according to similar underlying optical principles.
summary: This paper shows that instead of being purely detrimental, environmental dephasing can enhance light absorption in a ring of quantum emitters by moving population into long-lived subradiant modes. This mechanism is particularly effective with thermal dephasing and can lead to absorption efficiencies exceeding the sum of independent atoms.
main_problem: Investigating whether environmental decoherence, such as dephasing and phonon coupling, can be utilized as a resource to enhance single-photon absorption in subwavelength nano-rings of quantum emitters.
main_result: The study demonstrates that decoherence can enhance absorption by redistributing population from superradiant (bright) modes into subradiant (dark) modes, with thermal dephasing potentially allowing collective absorption to surpass that of independent emitters.
method: The authors use a Lindblad master equation approach within the single-excitation manifold, employing the Born-Markov approximation and analyzing steady-state populations and absorption cross-sections.
model_or_system: A nano-ring of N dipole-coupled two-level quantum emitters in the deep subwavelength regime, modeled with a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian including dipole-dipole interactions and collective radiative decay.
key_observables: Absorption cross-section, scattering cross-section, and excited state population.
important_parameters: Number of emitters (N), interparticle spacing (d/lambda_0), dephasing rates (pure and thermal), trapping rate (Gamma_T), and temperature (beta).
main_assumptions: Single-excitation manifold approximation, weak-field/weak-driving limit, Born-Markov approximation, and fixed emitter positions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the nano-ring geometry and the level scheme of collective modes; Figure 2 illustrates the absorption cross-section as a function of dephasing and emitter number, showing the emergence of enhancement.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of decoherence as a resource, defines the nano-ring model and master equation, analyzes single-atom vs. collective behavior under local dephasing, extends the study to thermal dephasing and temperature effects, and concludes with finite-size and incoherent illumination analysis.
why_interesting: It provides a counter-intuitive insight into open quantum systems, showing that environmental noise can be a constructive resource for enhancing light-matter interaction, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and light-harvesting research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the system. A nanoring of three-level quantum emitters (|g⟩, |e⟩and |t⟩) with radius R and interparticle spacing d, coupled through dipole–dipole interactions and driven by a classical field. Black arrows indicate the dipole moments; Ωand δ denote the Rabi frequency and detuning of a coherent drive. An emitter can decay from |e⟩into |g⟩by spontaneously emitting photons at rate Γ0, while it irreversibly decays into |t⟩at rate ΓT. (b) Level scheme of the single-excitation collective modes of the nano-ring, in the small-volume (Dicke) limit (R/λ0 ≪1). The bright mode (B) and the ground state (g) are coherently coupled by the external drive. Local and thermal dephasing,...

FIG. 2. Absorption cross-section σabs (in units of σ/4) in the presence of local (top panels) and thermal (bottom panels) dephasing, as a function of ΓT and the dephasing rate (ΓD or Γth B , respectively), normalized to the bright mode spontaneous decay rate NΓ0. (a) N = 1 (single emitter), where σabs decreases monotonically with ΓD/Γ0 and is maximal at ΓT = Γ0. (b) N = 10 and (c) N = 50. (d) βJ = 10−2, (e) βJ = 1 and (f) βJ = 102 for N = 10. All panels correspond to the Dicke (small-volume) limit. The dashed black line indicates the optimal dephasing rate maximizing σabs at fixed ΓT/NΓ0. σabs is always bounded by σ/4.

FIG. 3. Absorption cross section σabs (in units of σ/4) in pres- ence of thermal dephasing and in the Dicke (small-volume) limit, at fixed values of ΓT/NΓ0 = 0.6 and Γth B /NΓ0 = 0.05, plotted versus N and βJ.

FIG. 5. Scaling of the ratio σabs/Nσ1at abs with the number of emitters N, in the presence of thermal dephasing, for βJ = 3. For sufficiently large arrays (N ≳100), the ratio approaches a saturation value that depends on βJ. Inset: Saturation value of σabs/Nσ1at abs(Γ0), extracted for N = 300, as a function of βJ. The gray dashed line marks the asymptotic low temperature limit from Eq. (39), scaling as ∼Γ0/ΓT. The trapping rate and coupling strength to the bath are fixed to ΓT/Γ0 = 10−4

FIG. 4. Ratio of the collective absorption cross-section σabs of a nanoring to that of N independent emitters as a function of trapping (ΓT/NΓ0) and dephasing (ΓD/NΓ0 and Γth B /NΓ0) rates. (a) Pure local dephasing and (b) thermal dephasing with βJ = 1. In the independent emitter case, each emitter has spontaneous emission rate Γ0, trapping rate ΓT, and the dephasing rate is taken to be ΓD or Γth B , respectively. The dashed line marks 1, corresponding to equal absorption in the collective and independent cases. Results are shown for N = 10 in the Dicke (small-volume) limit.
